text: Philosopher Simone Weil wrote that attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity. She did not mean attention as vigilance but as willing receptivity — the suspension of the self's noise in order to hear what another is actually saying. Reading long-form literature trains precisely this capacity. The reader who inhabits a character's interiority for three hundred pages practices what Weil called "negative attention": setting aside one's own preconceptions to make room for an alien consciousness. This is not passivity but a disciplined emptying — closer in structure to contemplative prayer than to information retrieval.
question: What did Weil mean by "negative attention" in the context of reading?

options.text: Resisting distraction by strengthening willpower over time.
Critiquing a text rather than accepting its claims uncritically.
Setting aside one's own preconceptions to make room for an alien consciousness.
Focusing on the negative emotional content of a narrative.
